title: In "Punk'n Heads," the pumpkin masks are mandatory, the identity crisis is optional
author: Gareth Branwyn
summary: Dave Baker has a knack for making comics that feel like they're barely holding themselves together. His books blur formats, identities, and sometimes the boundaries of reality itself.
full_text:
Dave Baker has a knack for making comics that feel like they're barely holding themselves together. His books blur formats, identities, and sometimes the boundaries of reality itself. Mary Tyler MooreHawk did that through a kind of recursive media hallucination. His new book, Punk'n Heads, brings things crashing back to earth. Or at least down to the sticky floor of a punk club.

He's reunited here with Nicole Goux (This Place Will Kill Me) for a story about a horror-punk band that lives together, plays together, and as these things usually go, complicates it all by shagging together.

At the center of it all is Hannah Lipsky. She had a plan. She was going to be a fine artist. Then a breakup happened, she dropped out of school, landed in a flophouse, and somehow ended up fronting a band with silly costumes and a sketchy future. There's also an old crush in the mix, now a house- and bandmate, which is the kind of situation that rarely resolves itself in any healthy way, and both of them know it.

Baker has described the book as being for "broken hearted losers," the ones who thought they were headed somewhere charmed and instead find themselves loading second-hand gear into a shabby band bus, trying to figure it all out as they go along. "If you've ever broken up with someone in the back of a van, right before six idiots in Doc Martens are about to pile in, this is the book for you," adds Baker.

One of the book's funnier details: every Punk'n Head is required to wear a mask. It's a house rule, stated flatly, like a lease clause. The aesthetic sits somewhere between gimmick and genuine identity crisis. Clever branding? The band's history gets parceled out in dry little infographic asides, tracking the usual arc of DIY ambition, small wins, sudden lineup changes, and the constant low-grade pressure to become something legitimate and legible to an audience.

What makes this feel like a Baker book, even without the overt formal experimentation of MooreHawk, is the same underlying thematic concerns: who you thought you were, who you are when things fall apart, and how much of it all is just performative anyway.

Goux's art does a lot of the emotional heavy lifting. Working in a purple, blue, green, and yellow two-tone style, her line work has a loose, expressive quality that can phase-shift from funny to devastating in a single frame. The characters look slightly over-lived-in, like they've already picked up and put down a few versions of themselves.

Punk'n Heads is funny, dramatically messy, and probably uncomfortably on-the-nose for anyone who's ever tried to build a life around art, music, and the rapidly fading ideal of youthful authenticity.

 The book is out now from Top Shelf. Boing Boing was given a few pages to show off to our readers.
